content:
# Romans Chapter 10 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (א), 4th century CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/ROM_010_page_001.jpg

1. Brothers, the desire of the heart of me and the prayer to the God for them [is] for salvation.

2. For I bear witness to them that they have a zeal of God, but not according to knowledge.

3. For being ignorant of the righteousness of the God, and seeking to establish their own [righteousness], they did not submit to the righteousness of the God.

4. For [the] end of [the] law [is] Christos, for righteousness to everyone believing.

5. For Moses writes of the righteousness from the law, that "the human having done these things will live by them."

6. But the righteousness from faith speaks thus: "Do not say in the heart of you, 'Who will go up into the heaven?'" — that is, to bring Christos down —

7. or "'Who will go down into the abyss?'" — that is, to bring Christos up from [the] dead.

8. But what does it say? "The word is near you, in the mouth of you and in the heart of you" — that is, the word of the faith which we proclaim.

9. Because if you confess with the mouth of you [the] Lord Yeshua, and believe in the heart of you that the God raised him from [the] dead, you will be saved.

10. For with [the] heart [one] believes for righteousness, and with [the] mouth [one] confesses for salvation.

11. For the writing says, "Everyone believing upon him will not be put to shame."

12. For there is no distinction between Ioudaios and Greek; for the same Lord [is Lord] of all, being rich to all the [ones] calling upon him.

13. For "everyone who calls upon the name of [the] Lord will be saved."

14. How then will they call upon [him] in whom they did not believe? And how will they believe [in him] of whom they did not hear? And how will they hear without [one] proclaiming?

15. And how will they proclaim unless they are sent? Just as it has been written, "How beautiful [are] the feet of the [ones] proclaiming good news of good things."

16. But not all obeyed the good news. For Esaias says, "Lord, who believed the report of us?"

17. So the faith [is] from hearing, and the hearing through [the] word of Christos.

18. But I say, did they not hear? Indeed, "Into all the earth went out the sound of them, and into the ends of the inhabited [world] the words of them."

19. But I say, did Israel not know? First Moses says, "I will make you jealous by [a people that is] not a nation; by a nation without understanding I will anger you."

20. And Esaias is bold and says, "I was found by the [ones] not seeking me; I became manifest to the [ones] not asking for me."

21. But to Israel he says, "The whole day I stretched out the hands of me to a people disobeying and contradicting."

---
## EMET's Textual Notes

1. **v.4 — "End of the law" (τέλος νόμου):** τέλος can mean "termination" (the law is finished), "goal" (the law aimed at Christ), or "fulfillment" (Christ completes what the law intended). The ambiguity is likely intentional — Christ is both the goal toward which the law pointed and the end of the law as a system of justification.

2. **v.6-8 — Deuteronomy 30:12-14 adaptation:** Paul radically reinterprets Moses. In Deuteronomy, the "word" is the Torah; in Paul, the "word" is the gospel of Christ. Paul reads Christologically: you don't need to ascend to heaven (Christ has already come down) or descend to the abyss (Christ has already been raised). The word of faith is near — on your lips and in your heart.

3. **v.9 — The confession formula:** "Jesus is Lord" (κύριος Ἰησοῦς) — likely the earliest Christian creed. In the LXX, κύριος translates the divine name YHWH. To confess "Jesus is Lord" is to give Jesus the divine name.

4. **v.13 — Joel 2:32 quotation:** "Everyone who calls on the name of the Lord will be saved." In Joel, "Lord" is YHWH. Paul applies this to Jesus (v.9-12), implicitly identifying Jesus with YHWH.

5. **v.17 — "Word of Christ":** Sinaiticus reads ῥήματος Χριστοῦ ("word of Christ"). Some manuscripts read "word of God." The Sinaiticus reading connects hearing specifically to the message about Christ.

— EMET, AI Translator
